items.title: Use half-open intervals `[l, r)`
items.avoid: Mixing inclusive `[l, r]` with exclusive `[l, r)` in the same function.
items.why: Eliminates the +1/-1 noise and matches Python slicing semantics.
items.example:
```python
l = 0
for r in range(len(arr) + 1):    # r is exclusive
    window = arr[l:r]
```

items.title: Incrementally update the window state
items.avoid: Recomputing `sum(arr[l:r])` (or any O(k) aggregate) inside the loop.
items.prefer: Update on add (when r advances) and on remove (when l advances).
items.why: Re-aggregating per step kills the O(n) bound — back to O(n·k).
items.example:
```python
run = 0
for r in range(len(arr)):
    run += arr[r]
    while run > K:
        run -= arr[l]; l += 1
```

items.title: Shrink with `while`, not `if`
items.avoid: `if violated: l += 1` — only restores validity by one step.
items.why: Restoring validity may require shrinking many steps; a single `if` produces wrong answers on long violations.

items.title: Use a hash table to count window contents
items.avoid: Linear scans of the window for 'has duplicate?' or 'count of x'.
items.prefer: `collections.Counter` or a dict updated on add/remove.
items.why: Keeps each operation O(1); preserves the O(n) total bound.
items.example:
```python
from collections import Counter
c = Counter()
c[arr[r]] += 1
if c[arr[r]] > 1: ...
```

items.title: Reach for monotonic deque when you need rolling max/min
items.avoid: Sorting the window per step or using a heap with stale entries.
items.prefer: A `collections.deque` of indices kept monotonically decreasing (for max) or increasing (for min).
items.why: Each index is pushed and popped once → amortized O(1) per step, O(n) overall.
